data.title: North American Morning Briefing: Stock Futures Fall, Gold Hits Record on Fed Concerns -2-
data.description:
With five oil tankers seized-and more U.S. military action promised-the world's fleet of so-called shadow tankers that ferry sanctioned crude are quickly hoisting a new flag: the Russian tricolor.

Their hope is that by operating under the flag of a country with a strong navy they might be able to skirt the U.S. blockade on Venezuelan oil movements and avoid interception by the Coast Guard.

data.title: Sunway Launches $2.68 Billion Takeover Bid for IJM
data.description:
By Ying Xian Wong 




  KUALA LUMPUR, Malaysia--Malaysian conglomerate Sunway plans to acquire IJM Corp. in a $2.68 billion deal that would create the country's largest property and construction company. 



  Sunway is offering 3.15 ringgit for each IJM share under its conditional voluntary takeover offer for a total consideration of about 11 billion ringgit, the conglomerate said late Monday in a statement. The offer represents a premium of 14.55% to IJM's last closing price of 2.75 ringgit. 



  Sunway plans to issue new shares at 5.65 ringgit each to fund 90% of the consideration, and use cash for the remaining 10%. The cash consideration will be funded by debt financing and internal generated funds. 



  Sunway doesn't already hold any IJM Shares. Shares of both companies were suspended from trading Monday pending this material announcement. 



  Sunway said it expects to complete the deal by the third quarter. 



  The offer is conditional upon Sunway receiving valid acceptances resulting in more than 50% of IJM's voting shares, as well as relevant regulatory and shareholder approvals. If Sunway gets the nod for at least 90% of IJM's shares, it intends to exercise its rights of compulsory acquisition and proceed with the delisting of IJM from Bursa Malaysia. 



  A merger would create Malaysia's largest property and construction conglomerate by revenue and asset base, Sunway said, adding that it expects to enhance scale, create potential synergies and boost financing capabilities and credit strength. 



  "The proposed corporate exercise is timely and strategic to unlock synergies and value creation that extend beyond the group," Sunway President Anuar Taib said. 



  If the deal goes through, Sunway will continue IJM's existing businesses with no major changes planned, he said at a briefing. Sunway doesn't intend to lay off IJM employees, he added. 



  UBS AG Singapore Branch and Maybank Investment Bank are advisers on the offer. 





  Write to Ying Xian Wong at yingxian.wong@wsj.com 





  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  January 12, 2026 06:21 ET (11:21 GMT)


Copyright (c) 2026 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: India's Consumer Inflation Rose in December
data.description:
By Kimberley Kao and Jihye Lee 




  India's consumer-price growth picked up in December, inching closer to the central bank's forecast, which cited the inflationary backdrop as a factor in its decision to lower interest rates. 



  The consumer-price index rose 1.33% in December, compared with 0.71% in November, government data showed Monday, the highest monthly reading since September. 



  The print fell slightly short of the 1.60% increase forecast in a Wall Street Journal poll of economists. 



  The increase in inflation for personal care and effects, vegetables, and meat and fish products was a key driver of December's outturn, the statistics ministry said Monday. 



  Last month, the Reserve Bank of India resumed its rate-cutting cycle after pausing for two meetings, as the balance between price trends and growth--especially the benign inflation outlook--continued to provide room for monetary policy to support economic momentum, Gov. Sanjay Malhotra said. 



  Favorable base effects are fading, and the impact of elevated gold and silver prices is becoming more pronounced, ING economists said ahead of the data. Still, price pressures appear contained, which could prompt the RBI to consider additional rate cuts, they said. 



  The RBI raised its real gross domestic product growth forecast for the fiscal year ending in March to 7.3% from 6.8%, but it cut its full-year consumer inflation outlook to 2.0% from 2.6% projected in October. 





  Write to Kimberley Kao at kimberley.kao@wsj.com and Jihye Lee at jihye.lee@wsj.com 





  Corrections & Amplifications



  This article was corrected at 06:08 a.m. ET to clarify that India's December CPI reading marked the highest monthly reading since September 2025. An earlier article incorrectly said it was the highest since August. 



  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  January 12, 2026 06:06 ET (11:06 GMT)


Copyright (c) 2026 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: Banc of California Announces Schedule of Fourth Quarter 2025 Earnings Release and Conference Call
data.description:
Banc of California Announces Schedule of Fourth Quarter 2025 Earnings Release and Conference Call

Banc of California, Inc. (the “Company”) (NYSE: BANC) today announced it will release financial results for the fourth quarter and fiscal year ended December 31, 2025 after market close on Wednesday, January 21, 2026. The Company will host a conference call to discuss these financial results the following day on Thursday, January 22, 2026, at 10:00 a.m. Pacific Time (PT).

Interested parties are welcome to attend the conference call by dialing (888) 317-6003 and referencing event code 0299940. A link to the live audio webcast and the slide presentation for the call will be available on the Company’s investor relations website prior to the call. An audio archive of the conference call will be available on the Company’s investor relations website within 24 hours after the end of the call.

About Banc of California, Inc.

Banc of California, Inc. (NYSE: BANC) is a bank holding company with over $34 billion in assets and the parent company of Banc of California. Banc of California is one of the nation’s premier relationship-based business banks, providing banking and treasury management services to small-, middle-market, and venture-backed businesses. Banc of California is the largest independent bank headquartered in Los Angeles and the third largest bank headquartered in California and offers a broad range of loan and deposit products and services through 79 full-service branches located throughout California and in Denver, Colorado, and Durham, North Carolina, as well as through regional offices nationwide. The bank also provides full-service payment processing solutions to its clients and serves the Community Association Management industry nationwide with its technology-forward platform, SmartStreet™. The bank is committed to its local communities through the Banc of California Charitable Foundation, and by supporting organizations that provide financial literacy and job training, small business support, affordable housing, and more. Member FDIC. For more information, please visit us at www.bancofcal.com.

data.title: Precious-Metal Stocks Jump as Investors Seek Refuge in Gold and Silver
data.description:
By Adam Whittaker 




  Shares in silver and gold miners jumped as doubts over the independence of the Federal Reserve and geopolitical uncertainty triggered a flight to safety among investors, pushing precious metals to record highs. 



  Both metals have continued last year's rally, with gold rising 2.2% and hitting a record high of $4,600.40 a troy ounce. Silver surged 6.2% to $84.28 an ounce, having hit a record high of $84.69 earlier in the session. 



  The metals rose after Fed Chair Jerome Powell said he is under investigation by U.S. prosecutors. President Trump has long criticized Powell for what he calls an excessively cautious approach to monetary policy. 



  In morning trade in London, Fresnillo was up 6.7%, while Hochschild Mining rose 5.5%. Endeavour Mining ticked up 2.5%. In Johannesburg, Harmony Gold gained 7.6%, while Sibanye-Stillwater climbed 5.3%. 



  "This is about whether the Fed will be able to continue to set interest rates based on evidence and economic conditions, or whether instead monetary policy will be directed by political pressure or intimidation," Powell said in a video published Sunday. 



  Meanwhile, investors are watching protests in Iran and monitoring for signs the U.S. will launch military strikes against the regime in response to a harsh crackdown by authorities. 



  Iran is located near vital shipping lanes and military conflict there could squeeze global oil supply. The prosecution of Powell meanwhile raises concerns over the Fed's autonomy in monetary policy, and is putting downward pressure on the dollar. The dollar index--which measures the greenback against a basket of other major currencies--was down 0.4% to 98.75. Longer-dated Treasury yields rose after the news of Powell's prosecution, though short-dated yields fell. 



  Shares in precious-metal miners have rallied as the metals have become more expensive. Safe-haven demand due to geopolitical instability--which has also led to strong buying from central banks--helped drive the rallies in gold and silver. 



  Markets are responding to the unprecedented threat by the U.S. Justice Department to pursue a criminal indictment against Powell, MUFG's Soojin Kim wrote in a note. 





  Write to Adam Whittaker at adam.whittaker@wsj.com 





  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  January 12, 2026 06:02 ET (11:02 GMT)


Copyright (c) 2026 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: 'I'm on a fixed budget': Silver and gold are too rich for my blood. What else can I gift my adorable grandchildren?
data.description:
By Quentin Fottrell

'I've already listed them as beneficiaries on my 401(k)'

"I'm on a fixed budget, but I want them to have a little something." (Photo subject is a model.)

Dear Quentin,

What ideas do you have for setting something up for my grandchildren, ages 4 and 2? They don't sell savings bonds at banks anymore. Silver and gold bars are a little out of my price range. I'm on a fixed budget, but I want them to have a little something. I've already listed them as beneficiaries on my 401(k). I'm wondering what people usually do for their kids or grandchildren.

Grandmother

Don't miss: 'You need a third job or a rich husband': I fell victim to Allen Stanford's multibillion-dollar Ponzi scheme and survived

You can email The Moneyist with any financial and ethical questions at qfottrell@marketwatch.com. The Moneyist regrets he cannot reply to questions individually.

I will proceed on the basis that you can afford retirement with enough money to see you through to the final curtain, while being able to give your grandchildren a head start in life.

Dear Grandmother,

U.S. savings bonds are actually still available online, exclusively through the government website TreasuryDirect.gov. U.S. Series I Savings Bonds are an option - for Americans - through TreasuryDirect. There is a minimum purchase of $25. These bonds are inflation-protected and are quite popular with grandparents.

Showing your confidence in a young mind is priceless, whether it's with the gift of stocks or 529 plans for your grandchildren's education, particularly when the older generation did not have the same educational opportunities. You can keep it relatively simple, and even a few thousand dollars could help broaden their horizons later on.

You can give your grandchildren a gold-star education or teach them about compounding. You can also set up custodial accounts, opened under the Uniform Transfers to Minors Act or the Uniform Gifts to Minors Act. UGMAs typically contain securities, while UTMAs can hold other assets, including real estate.

You can keep it relatively simple, and even a few thousand dollars could help broaden their horizons later on.

With these types of accounts, you can take advantage of the annual gift-tax exclusion amount, which for 2025 and 2026 is $19,000 per person. The lifetime gift-tax exclusion will rise to $15 million next year from $13.99 million (but this will likely change over the next few years, as your grandchildren get older).

Any money you deposit in such an account is an irrevocable gift, meaning the funds have been removed from your estate, and your heirs will not benefit from a step-up in basis on these assets when you die. (Irrevocable trusts can contain spendthrift protections; UTMA assets cannot.) Be careful of "kiddie tax" exposure (where taxes are paid at your rate to prevent families from avoiding taxes).

The courts have ruled that 529 plans should be treated as gifts for tax purposes, even though they're in a parent's name. Setting up tax-advantaged 529 accounts for your grandchildren will do more than just help pay for their education, it will instill in them the expectation that they will have a college education.

Make sure you have funds set aside for the remainder of your life and for your spouse's life, too, if they are still with you, and create an emergency fund for unexpected expenses (should your house get damaged by a storm or you or your spouse need medical or long-term care). When you have run the numbers on your income and expenses, you can set up those 529s.

Don't miss: Silver, gold and copper are trouncing stocks. Here's what a key chart level suggests could be ahead for 2026.

Contributing to a 529 plan

You can contribute any amount to a 529, subject to your state's lifetime cap (usually several hundred thousand dollars). In 2026, recipients can use up to $20,000 per year for K-12 tuition, with expanded coverage for vocational and homeschooling expenses. You can also contribute up to the aforementioned annual gift-tax exclusion without having to report it to the IRS.

Just as with a 401(k) or IRA, those contributions add up. As Fidelity notes, a $50 monthly contribution could total more than $12,800 in 15 years; a $150 monthly contribution could reach more than $38,000 after 15 years; and a $250 monthly contribution could leave the recipient with nearly $64,000 after the same period of time.

These estimates show how a 529 might grow, assuming a 4.5% average annual return. They do not reflect an actual investment or account for taxes, fees, expenses or inflation, which would reduce returns, Fidelity says. Contributions to a 529 plan are made with after-tax dollars. As always, investments involve risk and past performance does not guarantee future results.

Showing your confidence in a young mind is priceless, whether it's with the gift of stocks or 529 plans.

When used for qualified expenses, 529 accounts allow you to take advantage of tax-deferred growth and federal income-tax-free withdrawals for education costs including college, university or vocational school tuition and related expenses, as well as K-12 education costs (up to $20,000 per year starting in 2026) and student-loan payments up to a lifetime limit of $10,000, Fidelity adds.

"About two-thirds (68%) of parents and one-fourth (23%) of grandparents surveyed by Fidelity said saving for their children's or grandchildren's future education is a top financial goal," the financial services company adds. "Yet many people surveyed choose not to set up a 529 plan account, instead setting aside college funds in a regular savings or brokerage account, if at all."

To equalize your grandchildren's inheritance later, you can adjust the distribution in your will or trust, reducing their shares by the amount you have already given each of them. A "hotchpot clause" in your will ensures fairness by effectively deducting the advance gifts from their inheritance share, retroactively calculating what each heir has already received.

You didn't provide your age or assets, so I will proceed on the basis that you can afford retirement with enough money to see you through to the final curtain, while being able to give your grandchildren a head start in life. It's a privilege and an opportunity to be able to make a difference in their lives, even long after you have left this world behind.

Don't miss: 'I'm terrified I'll be homeless when my husband, 76, stops working': We only have $100K. What happens to people like us?

More columns from Quentin Fottrell:

I want to gift my grandnieces, ages 5 and 10, stocks. How do I make sure they get an equal amount when they turn 18?

'So much for holiday cheer': I saw a guest take a bottle of wine as they left a Christmas party. Do I tell the host?

'Is it a case of them or us?' We're in our 40s. Do we retire early in our 50s - or save for our kids' education?

Check out the Moneyist private Facebook group, where we look for answers to life's thorniest money issues. Post your questions or weigh in on the latest Moneyist columns.

By submitting your story to Dow Jones & Co., the publisher of MarketWatch, you understand and agree that we may use your story, or versions of it, in all media and platforms.

-Quentin Fottrell

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires

data.title: Tata Consultancy Services Posts Lower Quarterly Profit
data.description:
By Kosaku Narioka 




  Tata Consultancy Services reported a decline in third-quarter net profit due to expenses related to new labor regulations in India and other one-off items. 



  The Indian information-technology services provider said Monday that net profit fell 14% from a year earlier to 106.57 billion rupees, equivalent to $1.18 billion, for the three months ended December. That missed the estimate of 128.16 billion rupees in a poll of analysts by data provider Visible Alpha. 



  Third-quarter revenue increased 4.9% to 670.87 billion rupees. 



  Profit from its banking, financial services and insurance segment rose to 68.61 billion rupees from 64.03 billion rupees.

data.title: 'I'm at a crossroads': I have $35K left on my mortgage at 3% interest. Do I dip into my $700K IRA to pay it off?
data.description:
By Quentin Fottrell

'Over the last year, my two retirement accounts averaged a 15% gain'

"It would be nice not to have to pay the $1,350 each month." (Photo subject is a model.)

Dear Quentin,

Is it smart to pay off my house?

I am 61 years old and have $50,000 in a 401(k) and $700,000 in a traditional IRA. There is $35,000 left on the house at 3% interest, and my monthly mortgage and escrow payment is $1,350.

Over the last year, my two retirement accounts averaged a 15% gain in the market. (I also have a $250,000 annuity, but I don't think I should count that as part of this decision.)

It would be nice not to have to pay the $1,350 each month. If I do, which account should I take the money from - the IRA or the 401(k)? I'm at a crossroads.

Should I pay off the house?

Nearing Retirement

You can email The Moneyist with any financial and ethical questions at qfottrell@marketwatch.com, and follow Quentin Fottrell on X, the platform formerly known as Twitter.

Don't miss: 'I'm terrified I'll be homeless when my husband, 76, stops working': We only have $100K. What happens to people like us?

Your 3% interest rate is just about in line with inflation, and you are putting the rest of your funds to much better work with the 15% return on your investments.

Dear Nearing,

All roads are pointing to the Emerald City.

Given the sum and interest rate involved, I'm inclined to leave your mortgage alone.

That said, I understand that finance is far more than just interest rates and dollar signs; it's also about peace of mind and the joy of living in a home that you own completely. Plus, you get to use that extra cash for other things, including - I hope - investing in your retirement.

Look at this way: Your 3% interest rate is just about in line with inflation, and you are putting the rest of your funds to much better work with the 15% return on your investments. Sure, we will all have down years. The S&P 500 SPX fell by 18% in 2022, but it has since more than recovered.

Withdrawals from a traditional 401(k) or IRA are considered ordinary income and are subject to your federal and, depending on where you live, state income tax rate. For a Roth IRA or Roth 401(k), withdrawals of contributions are tax-free.

Earnings are also tax-free and penalty-free if the account has been open for at least five years and you are over age 59 1/2, which you are. For that reason, you don't have to worry about paying extra fees. This is, for the most part, a luxury withdrawal.

The decision to pay off the house is yours, but you'll also have to take considerably more than $35,000 out of your retirement accounts, assuming that you need to pay tax on these. If you're in the 22% tax bracket, you'd have to take out closer to $45,000 to get that cash.

Higher tax bracket

For anyone at risk of pushing themselves into a higher tax bracket, it makes sense to withdraw some of that $45,000 in December, and the rest in January. Assuming you did not do that, you could take a smaller sum and overpay your mortgage.

If you decide to pay off your mortgage, consider taking the money from your IRA. Otherwise, you'd be effectively liquidating your 401(k). While 401(k)s can be more restrictive with investment choices, they also come with other benefits, such as employer matches (free money).

A member of the Moneyist Facebook Group, which mostly favors keeping the mortgage, has a compromise: "I would just accelerate your payments," they say. "Add enough to your payment every month that goes to principal to pay it off in a time frame like 24 or 36 months."

"By the time you're retired it's paid off but you're not taking a big lump sum out of any of your accounts," they add. "I overpay starting from day one when I have a mortgage or a car payment to accelerate payoff." You will also benefit psychologically from speeding up your progress.

It would be folly not to look at the opportunity cost of taking that $45,000 from your IRA. Over a 15-year period, that could cost you roughly $79,000 in today's dollars, assuming a 7% real return, or about $143,000 using the S&P 500's long-term average of around 10%.

Accelerating payments

Those figures, while sobering, reflect only the investment growth you give up by removing the money; they do not account for additional costs such as taxes on the withdrawal, or the impact of losing tax-deferred compounding.

Another Moneyist reader adds: "My house will be paid off in a couple of years, so I won't have a mortgage payment anymore. I will still have property taxes, maintenance, etc., but as I retire, that reduction in monthly expenses will allow me to live on a lower income."

"This ties into many people's concept of 'lifestyle,'" they add. "If someone is used to leasing cars, renting, and paying for subscription services - essentially a lifestyle of both needs and wants - then having a mortgage in retirement may be a non-issue."

"If, however, you base your lifestyle more on 'needs' and 'minimum costs,' then having the mortgage paid off becomes a higher priority. Your income while working, your income in retirement, and your overall cost of living all play a role in this as well."

OK, friend. Decision time. It seems important that you pay this off. It will set you psychologically free after years of paying your mortgage. If so, take the money from your traditional IRA and, ideally, spread the withdrawals over two (or more) tax years.

If this pushes you into a higher tax bracket, however, think again about paying it off. If all roads point to the Emerald City, where you are free of debt, take the one of least resistance.

A 3% interest rate is the kind of flying monkey that most people would want on their tail.

Related: 'How do I shield my retirement savings?' I'm worried about Trump's trade war and Fed's willingness to cut interest rates

The Moneyist regrets he cannot reply to questions individually.

More columns from Quentin Fottrell:

My therapist said he was in-network. He's not. Am I obliged to pay out-of-network rates for all prior sessions?

Can my husband contest his late brother's $600K will? He experienced oxygen deprivation due to COPD before he died.

I have early Alzheimer's and my husband has stage 4 kidney disease. We just inherited $50K. How can this help us?

Check out the Moneyist private Facebook group, where we look for answers to life's thorniest money issues. Post your questions or weigh in on the latest Moneyist columns.

-Quentin Fottrell

data.title: Chinese Solar, Battery Stocks Slide After Beijing Plans Export Tax Rebate Cuts—Update
data.description:
By Jiahui Huang and Jason Chau 




  Chinese solar stocks rose sharply after authorities unveiled plans to cancel export tax rebates on some solar products, a sector that has faced severe overcapacity, while battery stocks fell amid investor concerns over higher costs for overseas shipments following the policy move. 



  Shares of Trina Solar closed 8.6% higher in Shanghai, while Jinko Solar gained 3.6%. JA Solar Technology and Longi Green Energy Technology also ended the day up 3.4% and 4.6%, respectively. 



  Meanwhile, shares of Chinese battery giant CATL, listed in both mainland China and Hong Kong, fell by nearly 5% on Monday morning before closing more than 2% lower. Shares of smaller peers, including Eve Energy, Gotion High-Tech and Sunwoda Electronic, also declined. 



  The moves followed a statement Friday from China's Ministry of Finance saying export value-added tax rebates for solar and related products will be eliminated starting April 1. The rebate rate for battery and related products will be cut to 6% from 9% for the period from April 1 to Dec. 31. 



  Beijing has stepped up efforts in recent months to address overcapacity across parts of the Chinese economy, including the solar, battery and automobile sectors. 



  "We are not surprised about the policy adjustment, especially after the previous round of refund rate cut in 2024," Nomura analysts said in a note. China's total export value in the first 11 months of 2025 was $26 billion, including solar cells and modules, the analysts said. 



  In response, solar-module manufacturers could raise average selling prices in markets such as Europe, while export demand in regions including Southeast Asia may come under pressure, according to Nomura. 



  The analysts also said leading battery makers may pass the higher costs on to downstream customers, particularly in the U.S. and Europe, while mitigating the impact through localized production in export markets, such as CATL's new plant in Hungary. 



  "We believe the impact is more controllable given a longer policy transition period," they added. 



  China previously cut the export value-added tax rebates for solar and battery products to 9% from 13% on Dec. 1, 2024.
